text: While porous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) possess tremendous potential, their integration into electronic devices is generally very restricted, especially towards electrically conductive varieties. In this work, we demonstrate for the first time the applications of the new class of materials conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs as building blocks towards affordable, flexible and chemiresistive volatile organic compounds (VOCs) sensors. By controlling the structures of a series of chemically similar 2D MOFs with different electrical and catalytic characteristics while preserving their inherent structural functionality, and arranging them in a cross-reactive sensor array, excellent selectivities for different VOCs (volatile organic compounds) were achieved. Upon investigation, our experimental data confirm multiple orthogonal sensing mechanisms for every individual conductive 2D MOF, which signifies that 2D MOFs possess distinct characteristics compared with other conventional chemiresistive materials. This is in good harmony with the inherent advantages and unique functionality of the new class of material – the electrically conductive MOFs.
explanation: The text uses broad, marketing-like claims and polished but somewhat generic phrasing typical of AI-generated scientific prose. It lacks specific data details or experimental methods that usually anchor a real paper.
